questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of low back pain and stiffness. The pain is worse in the morning, lasting over an hour, and improves with exercise but not with rest. He has no history of trauma, fever, or weight loss. On examination, there is bilateral tenderness over the sacroiliac joints, and lumbar spine flexion is restricted. Schober's test is positive. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The key discriminating feature is inflammatory back pain: morning stiffness >30 minutes, improvement with exercise, worse with rest. This pattern, along with limited spinal flexion and sacroiliac tenderness, is classic for ankylosing spondylitis. Mechanical pain worsens with activity, infectious sacroiliitis would have fever and acute onset, fibromyalgia has widespread tenderness without specific spinal restriction, and disc prolapse typically presents with radicular pain.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents with bilateral Achilles tendon pain and swelling for 3 months. The pain is worst on waking and eases with gentle walking. He has no back pain, no preceding diarrhoea or urethritis, and no skin or nail changes. On examination, there is tenderness and swelling over both Achilles insertions, and he has a limp. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Bilateral Achilles enthesitis with morning stiffness (inflammatory pattern) in a young man is a classic presentation of ankylosing spondylitis, even without back pain. Plantar fasciitis is usually unilateral and not inflammatory; reactive arthritis follows an infection; gout is acute and monoarticular; psoriatic arthritis would have skin or nail involvement.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known ankylosing spondylitis, on regular naproxen, presents with a 2-day history of severe lower back and hip stiffness. He cannot get out of bed and has mild tenderness over both sacroiliac joints. Temperature is 37.5°C, and CRP is 40 mg/L. There is no radicular pain or neurological signs. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of known AS, bilateral sacroiliac tenderness, absence of fever >38°C, and mild CRP rise is most consistent with an acute flare. Septic arthritis or discitis would typically cause higher fever, severe localised pain, and unilateral involvement. Mechanical pain would not have such dramatic morning stiffness.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with recurrent episodes of red, painful right eye with photophobia. He has had three similar episodes in the past year, each lasting 2-3 weeks. Now he also reports low back pain and stiffness for several months. On examination, there is ciliary flush and cells in the anterior chamber of the right eye. Spinal flexion is reduced. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Recurrent acute anterior uveitis (red, painful eye with photophobia) is a classic extra-articular manifestation of ankylosing spondylitis. The accompanying inflammatory back pain confirms the diagnosis. Behçet's disease causes uveitis but also oral/genital ulcers; sarcoidosis often causes bilateral granulomatous uveitis; reactive arthritis follows infection; IBD-associated arthritis would have gastrointestinal symptoms.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 9-month history of low back pain and stiffness, worse in the morning and after rest, improving with activity. She also has bilateral knee and ankle swelling. There is no history of psoriasis, nail changes, diarrhoea, or mouth ulcers. On examination, she has limited lumbar spine flexion and tenderness over both sacroiliac joints. The knees show moderate effusions, and the ankles are tender. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: This woman has inflammatory back pain (typical of ankylosing spondylitis) with peripheral oligoarthritis (knees and ankles). In AS, peripheral arthritis is often asymmetric and affects lower limbs. Rheumatoid arthritis would involve symmetric small joints; psoriatic arthritis requires skin/nail changes; reactive arthritis follows infection; enteropathic arthritis requires IBD symptoms.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with chronic low back pain for 6 months. He reports morning stiffness lasting >30 minutes, improvement with exercise, and pain at night. Physical examination is normal. What is the most appropriate first investigation to confirm the diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis?
questions.explanation: In suspected ankylosing spondylitis, NICE recommends X-ray of the sacroiliac joints as the first-line imaging. MRI is reserved if X-ray is normal but clinical suspicion remains high. HLA-B27 is supportive but not diagnostic alone.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with a 3-year history of inflammatory back pain and reduced spinal mobility undergoes X-ray of the sacroiliac joints, which shows bilateral grade 2 sacroiliitis. What is the correct interpretation of this result?
options: It is non-specific and requires HLA-B27 testing
It confirms the diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis
It indicates advanced disease requiring urgent treatment
questions.explanation: According to the modified New York criteria, bilateral grade 2 sacroiliitis on X-ray along with clinical features (inflammatory back pain, reduced spinal mobility) is sufficient to diagnose ankylosing spondylitis. No further imaging is needed.

questions.stem: A 27-year-old man presents with acute onset of severe low back pain and stiffness for 2 weeks. He has had similar episodes in the past and also reports a history of heel pain. X-ray of the sacroiliac joints is normal. What is the most appropriate next investigation to confirm a diagnosis of ankylosing spondylitis?
options: CT scan of the sacroiliac joints
questions.explanation: When there is high clinical suspicion of axial spondyloarthritis but X-ray is normal, MRI of the sacroiliac joints is the recommended next-step investigation to detect active inflammation or structural changes. HLA-B27 may support diagnosis but does not confirm.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with recurrent episodes of acute anterior uveitis is referred to rheumatology for possible underlying ankylosing spondylitis. He has no back pain. What is the most appropriate investigation to establish the diagnosis?
questions.explanation: In a patient presenting with recurrent uveitis, a common extra-articular manifestation of spondyloarthritis, X-ray of the sacroiliac joints is the first-line screening test for sacroiliitis, even in the absence of back pain.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with inflammatory back pain for 3 months has an X-ray showing unilateral grade 2 sacroiliitis. According to the modified New York criteria, which of the following best describes the diagnostic significance of this finding?
options: It is diagnostic of ankylosing spondylitis when combined with clinical features
It is insufficient alone; must also have bilateral involvement or unilateral grade 3-4
It is diagnostic only if HLA-B27 positive
It requires MRI to confirm active inflammation
questions.explanation: The modified New York criteria require radiological sacroiliitis of at least bilateral grade 2-4 or unilateral grade 3-4. Unilateral grade 2 does not meet the radiological criterion, so further imaging (e.g., MRI) or follow-up is needed.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with 6 months of inflammatory back pain and morning stiffness lasting over an hour. MRI shows active sacroiliitis. CRP is 22 mg/L. He has no peripheral joint involvement or enthesitis. What is the most appropriate first-line pharmacological therapy?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend NSAIDs as first-line therapy for active axial ankylosing spondylitis without peripheral arthritis. DMARDs and biologics are reserved for inadequate response to NSAIDs.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known ankylosing spondylitis has persistent swelling and tenderness of both knees and ankles despite adequate trials of NSAIDs. She has minimal axial symptoms. What is the most appropriate next pharmacological therapy?
questions.explanation: For peripheral arthritis in AS, sulfasalazine is the recommended first-line DMARD. Methotrexate lacks strong evidence in AS. Biologics are considered if DMARDs fail or for axial disease.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with known ankylosing spondylitis presents with a red, painful, photophobic left eye. Slit-lamp examination confirms acute anterior uveitis. What is the most appropriate immediate therapy?
questions.explanation: Acute anterior uveitis (iritis) in AS is typically treated with topical corticosteroids as first-line. Oral steroids are reserved for severe or bilateral cases. Methotrexate and biologics are used for chronic or refractory uveitis.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis has a T-score of -3.0 at the femoral neck on DXA scan. He has no prior fractures. What is the most appropriate pharmacological therapy for his osteoporosis?
questions.explanation: NICE recommends bisphosphonates (e.g., alendronate) as first-line treatment for osteoporosis in men. Denosumab is second-line; teriparatide for severe cases; raloxifene and HRT are for postmenopausal women only.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with ankylosing spondylitis is planning pregnancy. She currently takes methotrexate 15 mg weekly for peripheral arthritis. Which of the following drugs is contraindicated in pregnancy?
questions.explanation: Methotrexate is teratogenic and contraindicated in pregnancy. Sulfasalazine, prednisolone, and etanercept (with caution) can be used. NSAIDs are avoided in the third trimester but not absolutely contraindicated throughout.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man presents with chronic low back pain and stiffness for 6 months, worse in the morning and after rest, improving with exercise. He has no peripheral arthritis or uveitis. CRP is 20 mg/L and HLA-B27 is positive. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: NICE guidelines recommend referral to rheumatology for suspected axial spondyloarthritis (AxSpA) in patients with chronic back pain starting before age 45, especially with inflammatory features and a positive HLA-B27. While MRI may be diagnostic, the immediate next step is referral for specialist assessment to confirm diagnosis and initiate appropriate management.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with known ankylosing spondylitis, well-controlled on naproxen, now presents with bilateral knee swelling and dactylitis of the second toe. CRP is 45 mg/L. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Peripheral arthritis in ankylosing spondylitis is managed with a DMARD; sulfasalazine is the first-line option as per NICE. NSAID dose increase may be insufficient, and TNF inhibitors are reserved for inadequate response to conventional DMARDs. Joint aspiration is not indicated without suspicion of septic arthritis.

questions.stem: A 40-year-old man with ankylosing spondylitis on adalimumab presents with acute severe low back pain, fever of 39°C, and inability to walk. CRP is 200 mg/L. What is the single most appropriate next step?
options: Perform urgent MRI of the spine
questions.explanation: The combination of fever and severe pain in a patient on TNF inhibitor raises concern for spinal infection (discitis/osteomyelitis) rather than a flare. Urgent MRI is essential to differentiate infection from inflammation before considering immunosuppression escalation. Antibiotics may be needed later but imaging is the immediate priority.

questions.stem: A 50-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis and known restrictive lung disease presents with sudden-onset dyspnoea and pleuritic chest pain. His oxygen saturation is 90% on room air. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Acute dyspnoea with pleuritic chest pain in a patient with restricted mobility from AS raises high suspicion for pulmonary embolism. CT pulmonary angiogram is the definitive investigation. Chest X-ray and ECG may be done but CTPA should not be delayed; oxygen can be given concurrently but imaging is the priority.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman with ankylosing spondylitis on etanercept is planning pregnancy and asks about the safety of her medication. What is the single most appropriate next step?
questions.explanation: Management of biologics in pregnancy requires specialist counselling. While certolizumab is considered safer, individualised decisions based on disease activity and risk are needed. Stopping abruptly may cause flare; continuing without discussion is not advised. Referral to rheumatology is the appropriate next step for shared decision-making.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old man with established ankylosing spondylitis presents to the emergency department with acute severe neck pain after slipping on ice and landing on his back. He has no head injury. On examination, there is tenderness over the cervical spine and muscle spasm. Which single finding would indicate a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate imaging?
options: Reduced neck flexion to 60% of normal
Loss of cervical lordosis on inspection
questions.explanation: New-onset urinary retention indicates possible cauda equina syndrome or spinal cord compression from an unstable spinal fracture, which is a life-threatening emergency requiring urgent MRI. Loss of lordosis and reduced range are common in AS without fracture; bilateral arm paraesthesia is non-specific; occipital pain is common from muscle spasm.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with long-standing ankylosing spondylitis presents with recurrent syncopal episodes. He feels lightheaded and has had two episodes of collapse in the past week. His heart rate is 38 bpm and irregular. Which single ECG finding most likely explains his presentation and requires immediate intervention?
options: Atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response
questions.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis is associated with aortitis and fibrosis of the conducting system, causing complete heart block, which presents with syncope and bradycardia and requires urgent pacing. First-degree block and sinus bradycardia are less acute; LBBB and slow AF are possible but less specific and not as immediately life-threatening.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man with known ankylosing spondylitis presents with a sudden onset of severe right eye pain, blurred vision, and headache. On examination, the eye is red. Which single finding would most suggest a life-threatening emergency?
questions.explanation: A fixed, mid-dilated pupil is hallmark of acute angle-closure glaucoma, a vision-threatening emergency requiring immediate ophthalmology referral. Ciliary flush, hypopyon, and photophobia are typical of anterior uveitis, which is common in AS but not an acute emergency in isolation. Corneal oedema can occur but is less specific.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with a 6-month history of lower back pain and morning stiffness that improves with exercise. He is otherwise well. Which single finding on history or examination would raise suspicion of a different, more urgent diagnosis such as infection or malignancy?
options: A history of alternating buttock pain
Nocturnal pain that wakes him from sleep
questions.explanation: Nocturnal pain that wakes the patient is a red flag for infection (discitis) or malignancy, whereas morning stiffness, alternating buttock pain, and reduced spinal mobility are typical of inflammatory back pain in AS. Family history is a risk factor, not a red flag.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with severe ankylosing spondylitis has developed progressive kyphosis and recent onset of difficulty swallowing. She also reports a sensation of pins and needles in her fingertips. Which single finding on examination would indicate an immediate need for cervical spine imaging to exclude atlantoaxial subluxation?
options: Loss of cervical lordosis on palpation
Limited cervical rotation to 45 degrees bilaterally
questions.explanation: Lhermitte sign (electric shock on neck flexion) is a classic sign of cervical cord compression, often from atlantoaxial subluxation in advanced AS, and warrants urgent MRI. Gait unsteadiness can indicate myelopathy but is less specific. Loss of lordosis and limited rotation are common in AS. Thenar wasting suggests carpal tunnel syndrome, not cord compression.
